(9) I knew they were my Christmas presents.

(11) My mother carefully opened the gifts.
(12) Wow!
(13) I saw ice skates, red leather gloves and a dollhouse.
(14) The last present was the music box we'd asked the lady at the local store to take down and play for us over and over again.
(15) "Which ones?"
(16) my mother asked.
(17) "She can have the ice skates," I said reluctantly .
(18) "That's good," my mother said.
(19) She began to tape the packages up again.
(20) Then she stopped and asked, "Are you sure about these?"
(21) At that moment, I realized she expected more from me than I had already given.
(22) So I gave her the music box, too, and smiled.
(23) The next morning, Marley's eyes were resolute .

(26) I thought my happiness would be cut in half.
(27) Instead it was doubled.
(28) Over the last 40 years, Marley and I have grown apart .

(30) Today Marley says, "They were the best presents I ever got.

(1) The word, "photography", was first used in 1839.

(10) Back in 1872, people didn't know exactly whether all four of a horse's hooves   left the ground at the same time when it was running.

(12) Most people believed that a horse always had one hoof on the ground, or it would fall over.
(13) But Stanford didn't think so.
(14) At that time, it was hard to know who could win the bet, because a horse's legs move so fast that it is impossible to tell just by looking.
(15) So they needed a way to record the movement of a running horse.
(16) Then Stanford offered $25,000 to the famous photographer, Muybridge, to help find the answer.
(17) In the beginning, Muybridge failed to get clear images, but he didn't give up.
(18) He continued to improve his cameras.
(19) In 1878, after many experiments, he managed to get a sequence   of 12 photos.
(20) One of them clearly showed that all four of the horse's hooves were off the ground at the same time.
(21) And when the photos moved fast, people could see a horse running.
(22) Though is usually considered as the person who created the first movie in 1889, it was the work of Eadweard Muybridge and the bet that led to Edison's invention.

a|true|high6620.txt.passage:
(1) I was now in my twenty third year of residence in this island and was accustomed to the place and to the manner of living.
(2) If it had not been the savages who had come to the place to disturb me, I could have been content to spent the rest of my time there, even to the last moment, till I had laid me down and died, like the old goat in the cave.
(3) I had also arrived to some little recreations and amusements, which made the time pass more pleasantly with me a great deal than it did before.
(4) At first, I had taught my Poll to speak.
(5) And he did it so familiarly and talked so clearly and plain that it was very pleasant to me.
(6) And he lived with me no less than twenty six years.
(7) How long he might live afterwards, I don't know; though I know they have an idea in Brazil that they live a hundred years.
(8) Perhaps poor Poll may be alive there still, calling Poor Robin Crusoe to this day.
(9) I wish no other English man had the bad luck to go there and hear him.
(10) But if he did, he would certainly believe it was the devil .
(11) My dog was a very pleasant and loving companion to me, for no less than sixteen years of my time, and then died of mere old age.
(12) As for my cats, they multiplied to that degree that I had to shoot several of them at first to keep them from eating up all I had.
(13) Besides these, I had two more parrots which talked pretty well and would all call Robin Crusoe, but none like my first.
(14) Nor indeed did I take the pains with any of them that I had done with him.
(15) I had also several sea-fowls, whose names I don't know, that I caught upon the shore and cut their wings.
(16) And the little stakes which I had planted before my castle wall being now grown up to a good thick bush, these fowls all lived among these low trees and bred there, which was very agreeable to me; so that as I said above, I began to be well content with the life I led if not worried about the threat from the savages.

a|true|high22428.txt.passage:
(1) Starting college can be a difficult experience.
(2) You have to deal with new responsibilities, growing independence, a challenging course load, and of course, the social scene.
(3) When a roommate is thrown into the mix, it may feel like you're struggling with all that stuff while living in a small box with a stranger.
(4) But having a roommate doesn't need to be one more thing to worry about.
(5) When students go into their living situations with realistic expectations and a willingness to compromise, things can work out just fine.
(6) When you first meet your roommate, chances are that you'll be on your best behavior.
(7) You want to get along since this is the person who's going to be sharing your living space for the next year.
(8) But try to think ahead to potential worst cases, too.
(9) For example, imagine it's 2 am and you're working on a paper that's due in 8 hours.
(10) Your roommate comes in from a party and wants to continue the party in your room.
(11) At times like these, you can't be feeling good.
(12) That's why talking about issues that might come up ahead of time and respecting each other's wishes when the time comes is so important.
(13) Talk about the things that are really important to you, and make sure your roommate understands.
(14) Then encourage him or her to do the same.
(15) For instance, does it make you angry when people take things without asking first?
(16) Let your roommate know your feelings about these types of things from the start.
(17) There are some things to talk to your roommate about so that both of you have a feel for each other's likes, dislikes and habits.

a|true|high6317.txt.passage:
(1) Forget Twitter and Facebook, Google and the Kindle.
(2) Television is still the most influential medium around.
(3) Indeed, for many of the poorest regions of the world, it remains the next big thing--finally becomes globally available.
(4) And that is a good thing, because the TV revolution is changing lives for the better.
(5) Across the developing world, around 45% of families had a TV in 1995; by 2005 the number had climbed above 60% .
(6) That is some way behind the U.S. ,where there are more TVs than people, and where people now easily get access to the Internet.
(7) Five million more families in sub-Saharan Africa will get a TV over the next five years.
(8) In 2005 , after the fall of the Taliban ,which had banned TV, I in 5 Afghans had one.
(9) The global total is another 150 million by 2013--pushing the numbers to well beyond two thirds of families.
(10) Television's most powerful effect will be on the lives of women.
(11) In India, researchers Robert Jensen and Emily Oster found that when TVs reached villages, women were more likely to go to the market without their husbands approval and less likely to want a boy rather than a girl.
(12) They were more likely to make decisions over child health care.
(13) TV is also a powerful medium for adult education.
(14) In the Indian state of Gujarat, Chitrageet is a popular show that plays Bollywood songs with words in Gujarati on the screen.
(15) Within six months, viewers had made a small but significant improvement in their reading skills.
(16) Too much TV has been associated with violence, overweight and loneliness.
(17) However, TV is having a positive influence on the lives of billions worldwide.

a|true|high6081.txt.passage:
(1) Italy is one country where beauty is prized more than any other virtue.
(2) That is, except in the small town of Piobbico, the self-declared World Capital of Ugly People.
(3) The road sign at the edge of the town even warns visitors that they are entering the ugly zone.
(4) People who consider themselves ugly have been gathering in Piobbico since the 1960s.
(5) That's when Ugly Club president Telesforo Lacobelli established a dating agency for women who believed they were too ugly to attract husbands.
(6) Lacobelli believes that he is ugly himself because he has a short nose in a country where long or large noses have always been considered beautiful.
(7) People from around the world travel to Piobbico to tell their sad stories of ugliness.
(8) During the annual Festival of the Ugly, which occurs on the first Sunday of every September, hundreds of people gather in Piobbico's town square to elect the president of the Ugly Club.
(9) Lacobelli wins the election every year.
(10) The Ugly Club has over 20, 000 members.
(11) They carry ID cards that grade their ugliness from bearable to extreme.
(12) A prize is awarded to Ugly Club members who qualify as extremely ugly.
(13) The Ugly Club president insists that ugliness is a virtue.
(14) Since beautiful people get a lot of attention for their beauty alone, they have to work hard to prove their other virtues.
(15) Ugly people, on the other hand, are genuine and do not have to prove anything to anybody, according to Lacobelli.
(16) Lacobelli is a spokesperson for ugly people everywhere.
(17) He believes that the uglier one is, the better life can be.
(18) Though the club enjoys making fun of beauty, especially beauty contests, Lacobelli has a serious side as well.
(19) He believes that too many people suffer from financial and emotional pressures because they don't meet society's standards of beauty.
(20) The fact that beautiful people are more successful in the workforce is a problem that Lacobelli has attempted to bring forward to the Italian public and government.

a|true|high10087.txt.passage:
(1) When we say older people shrink, we don't mean they become tiny enough to fit in your pocket!
(2) We just mean that it's common for older people to become a little shorter over time.
(3) This kind of shrinking can't be reversed, although people can slow or stop this process.
(4) But why does shrinking happen at all?
(5) Because of gravity (the force that keeps your feet on the ground) , cushions between the bones in the spine  , get compressed  , which makes a person lose a little height and become shorter.
(6) Another reason why some older people shrink is because of osteoporosis  .
(7) Osteoporosis occurs when bone is broken down and not enough new bone material is made.
(8) Over time, bone is said to be lost because it's not being replaced.
(9) Bones become smaller and weaker and can easily break if someone with osteoporosis is injured.
(10) Older people--especially women, who generally have smaller and lighter bones to begin with--are more likely to develop osteoporosis.
(11) As years go by, a person with osteoporosis can get small breaks in bones that are called compression fractures .
(12) These breaks cause collapse of the spine and over time the person with osteoporosis can become hunched over  .
(13) Did you know that every day you shrink a little, too?
(14) You aren't as tall at the end of the day as you are at the beginning.
(15) That's because as the day goes on, water in the disks of the spine gets compressed due to gravity, making you just a tiny bit shorter.
(16) Don't worry, though.
(17) Once you get a good night's rest, your body recovers, and the next morning, you're standing tall again!
(18) Smoking and drinking alcohol can make this problem worse.
(19) Want to do something right now to build strong bones?
(20) I believe what I will say will help you.

a|true|high796.txt.passage:
(1) Most people consider skydiving   a product of the 20th century, but its history actually goes further back than that.
(2) The Chinese attempted to parachute   in the 10th century, a thousand years before we did.
(3) The Chinese did what we would today call base diving; that is, they jumped off a place that would allow them to float from a height to the ground.
(4) The first person to attempt to parachute was a Frenchman named Jacques Garnerin.
(5) He jumped from his hot-air balloon at the end of the 18th century and did tricks on the way down and amazed the crowds by landing safely on the ground.
(6) At the end of the 19th century, Kathie Paulus, a brave German woman, became famous for her skydiving skills.
(7) Once the airplane was invented, skydiving took on a whole new form.
(8) The airplane made it possible to dive from greater heights at greater speeds, allowing for more range in the movements in the air.
(9) A woman named Tiny Broadwick became the first woman to jump from a plane in 1913 and dive free fall in 1914.
(10) Skydiving was not called skydiving until the middle of the 1950s, when Ronald Young invented the word.
(11) It had been called parachuting before this and was mainly used by the military   to land troops in inland locations, or for pilots to jump out of their planes when necessary.
(12) Once World War I was over, parachuting became a sport, which we now call skydiving.
(13) After World War II, this activity became more a hobby than a military action.
(14) Soldiers were trained in parachuting and enjoyed the thrill so much that they continued on for fun.
(15) From this, teams and competitions were formed.
(16) Skydiving schools appeared in the late 1950s and now it is a recognized extreme sport enjoyed by many.

a|true|high20111.txt.passage:
(1) Where is that noise coming from?
(2) Not sure?
(3) Try living with your eyes closed for a few years.
(4) Blind people are better at locating sounds than people who can see, a new study says, without the benefits of vision the ears seem to work much better.
(5) Previous studies have shown that blind people are better than others at reaching out and touching the sources of sounds that are close by.
(6) Researchers from the University of Montreal wanted to see if blind people were also better at locating sounds that are far away.
(7) Twenty-three blind people participated in the study.
(8) All had been sightless for at least 20 years.
(9) Fourteen of them had lost their vision before age 11. the rest went blind after age 16.
(10) The experiment also included 10 people who could see but were wearing blind-folds.
(11) In one task, volunteers had to pick the direction of a sound coming from about 3 metres away.
(12) When the sound was in front of them or slightly off center in front, both groups performed equally well.
(13) When sounds came from the side or the back, however, the blind group performed much better than the blindfolded group.
(14) The participants who had been blind since childhood did slightly better than those who lost their sight later.
(15) Recognizing the locations of distant sounds can be a matter of life-or-death for blind people, say the researchers.
(16) Crossing the street, for instance, is much harder when you can't see the cars coming.
(17) Still, the researchers were surprised by how well the blind participants did, especially those who went blind after age 16.
(18) In another experiment, the scientists also found that parts of the brain that normally deal with visual information became active in locating sound in the people who were blind by age 11.
(19) These brain parts didn't show sound-location activity in the other group of blind people or in the sighted people.
(20) The scientists now want to learn more about the working of brains of "late-onset" blind people.

cnt_evidence:

   - The statement claims that the ability to locate sounds is of vital importance for the blind.

3. Evaluate the Evidence
   - Sentence (15) and (16) both confirm that the ability to locate sounds is of vital importance for the blind, as it can impact their safety and survival in various situations.

4. Check Sufficiency
   - Sentence (15) and (16) together provide clear evidence of the importance of locating sounds for the blind.
   - Sentence (15) directly states the importance, while Sentence (16) provides a concrete example.

5. Anaphora Resolution
   - Sentence (15) uses no pronouns but refers to "blind people," which is introduced earlier in the passage.
   - Sentence (16) uses the pronoun "you" to refer to the blind, but the context makes it clear that the statement is about blind people in general.

6. Conclusion
   - Sentence (15) and (16) both support the statement that the ability to locate sounds is of vital importance for the blind.
   - Both sentences are needed to provide a clear and comprehensive understanding of the statement's truth.

a|true|high3166.txt.passage:
(1) Hundreds of students from around the world gathered in New York City last week for the Microsoft Imagine Cup finals.
(2) They came to present their ideas for using technology to solve world problems.
(3) Microsoft education director Suzi Levine says the nine-year-old program began mainly as a competition to create technology.
(4) SUZI LEVINE: "When we realized that students really actually want to have a purpose for what they're creating, we introduced the idea of inspiring them with the UN Millennium Development Goals and suggesting that they use those for their muse .
(5) "This past year we also rolled out something called the Imagine Cup Solve This library , where IGOs, NGOs and nonprofits can submit some of the technical challenges that they would like students to consider for their solutions."
(6) Microsoft says over 350,000 high school and college students registered for this year's competition.
(7) Judges chose more than four hundred of them to attend the finals.
(8) SUZI LEVINE: "One from Thailand was called NewKrean, where they created a Windows Phone 7 application that allows you to broadcast your location to your social network of friends so that you can be more easily rescued."
(9) They named their application Terra.
(10) Suzi Levine says there were also ideas from Egypt inspired by the revolution that removed president Hosni Mubarak in February.
(11) SUZI LEVINE: "One was to use Bluetooth as sort of a Twitter equivalent so that if the government shuts down the Internet, you actually can still have a massive social distribution."
(12) Students competed in nine categories.
(13) For example, in software design the top prize of twenty-five thousand dollars went to Team Hermes from Ireland.
(14) The students developed a device for cars to collect information on road conditions, driving behavior and traffic incidents.
(15) A team from Taiwan's National Tsing Hua University won first place in the embedded  development category.
(16) They developed a network of wireless devices to help plot the safest escape routes during a fire.
(17) Next year's awards ceremony will take place in Australia.
(18) Registration for Imagine Cup twenty-twelve opened Friday.
(19) Also, Microsoft announced plans for a three million dollar program to help Imagine Cup winners further develop their projects.

(14) "But the problem is that we're only able to look at associations and aren't yet able to conduct cause-and-effect studies."
(15) In the clinical study, researchers found a growth of too much endotoxin-producing bacteria, leading t0 35 percent ofthe gut bacteria, in an obese patient whose initial weight was 175 kg.
(16) Based on this information, researchers tried to cure the patient by feeding him a specialized nutritional liquid food to decrease the bacteria in his guts to ignorable amounts.
(17) After 23 weeks, the patient lost 51.4 kg, with his fatty liver disease having almost disappeared.

(7) *If someone tries to follow you, make for a public place, such as a shop or a restaurant or walk towards other people.
(8) Make lots of noise and use your personal alarm to shock the person and use this opportunity to make your escape.
(9) If you need to use your mobile phone on route go into a shop or stand with your back to a shop window and constantly survey the area around you.
(10) Keep the conversation short and to the point and try not to be too obvious doing it.
(11) *Walk with confidence and look as though you're on a mission.
(12) Keep your head up and while you should look ahead to avoid unnecessary conflicts, such as a group of young men a-head of you, you should also be aware of everything around you.
(13) Whenever possible use well lit and busy streets and definitely avoid alleyways, wasteland and other quiet short cuts.
(14) If you have any information relating to these crimes, please call Crime Stoppers on 0800 555 111 or call Thames Valley Police on 101 for non-emergency reporting.

a|true|high3459.txt.passage:
(1) If you like bicycling as much as you like playing ball and can't seem to decide between the two sports, you may want to consider cycle ball--a fun sport that adopts the key attributes from both activities and transforms it into a totally new game.
(2) Also known as red ball, the game was invented in 1893 by German laborers who wished to play polo , but could not afford horses.
(3) While the sport has not caught on in North America, it is very fashionable in Europe and even Japan.
(4) Played in an indoor court, cycle ball is similar to soccer in many ways--the aim is to score as many goals as possible, and only the goalkeeper is allowed to touch the ball.
(5) The one big difference?
(6) Use of legs is forbidden.
(7) Instead, players have to guide the ball across the court, using the front wheels of their bicycles or with their heads.
(8) In fact, if a player even touches the floor with his feet during the 14-minute game, it is considered a foul  and the opposing team gets a free kick.
(9) While the game was initially  played with normal bikes, things are a little different now.
(10) The seats of these specialized bikes are positioned over the back tire and they sport a single fixed gear, making it easier for the rider to control the constant back and forth movement required for the game.
(11) The handlebars  also point straight up so that the competitors can sit upright while playing and they are about twice the weight of normal bikes, which helps with the stability.
(12) While cycle ball may sound a little strange, it is a fast-paced sport that requires much training, skill, and, is also really fun to watch.

(1) What's your idea of a good time?
(2) What about dancing in a rainy field with one hundred and fifty thousand other people while a famous rock band plays on a stage so far away that the performers look like ants?
(3) It may sound strange but that is what many hundreds of thousands of young people in the UK do every summer.
(4) Why?
(5) Because summer is the time for outdoor music festivals.
(6) Held on a farm, the Glastonbury Festival is the most well-known and popular festival in the UK.
(7) It began in 1970 and the first festival was attended by one thousand five hundred people each paying an admission price of PS1 -- the ticket included free milk from the farm.
(8) Since then the Glastonbury Festival has gone from strength to strength -- in 2004 one hundred and fifty thousand fans attended, paying PS112 each for a ticket to the three-day event.
(9) Tickets for the event sold out within three hours.
(10) Performers included superstars, such as Paul McCartney and James Brown, as well as new talent, like Franz Ferdinand and Joss Stone.
(11) Although many summer festivals are run on a profit-making basis, Glastonbury is a charity event, donating millions of pounds to local and international charities.
(12) Glastonbury is not unique  in using live music to raise money to fight global poverty.

a|true|high3645.txt.passage:
(1) Job advertising on the web
(2) Register a new account
(3) Log in to an existing account
(4) Promote your casual work, graduate or internship ,opportunity with an advertisement on the Careers Centre database free of charge.
(5) Your advertisement will be viewable to all university of Sydney students and appear in 'Job Alert' - a daily email to subscribing students.
(6) You can also target alumni (up to five years after graduation) - advertise on our database and it will be visible at AlumniOnline.
(7) If you advertise through the Careers Centre you will need to agree to our advertising terms and conditions.
(8) Unpaid work
(9) We are unable to advertise unpaid internship work experience or volunteer positions unless: the position fulfills part of a University curriculum and has faculty approval, or it is a volunteer position with a registered not-for-profit organisation.
(10) Our unpaid and volunteering work info brochure has essential information for employers wishing to offer unpaid work including alternative suggestions.
(11) You can also contact the Careers Centre for assistance.
(12) Submit an advertisement
(13) To upload your job advertisement: log in to the Careers Centre employer database - if you don't have a username you'll need to request a new account, click 'job ads' and fill out the form, agree to our advertising terms and conditions, submit your advertisement for review by the Careers Centre.
(14) Advertisements are displayed for a maximum of two months.
(15) If your position is filled before your registered deadline please log in to the database and withdraw your ad.
(16) What to include in your advertisement
(17) We've found there is generally a better response to advertisements that are clear and informative.
(18) The following information is important to include: job title and description of duties, skills and/or experience required, hours worked, and flexibility with regard to study/exam timetables, location of the work, pay rate/basis of payment - visit the Fair Work online website for pay rates and other employment conditions, or call their help line on 131394, how to apply and close date for applications, your contact details, including email address and phone number for Careers Centre staff (these do not appear with the job advertisement, however they are required for office purposes should we need to contact you).
(19) Cost
(20) It is free to advertise on our jobs database.
(21) Related services
(22) For greater promotion of your opportunities, consider: sending a targeted email-out to the students you wish to recruit( ), booking a booth at a careers fair if you are promoting a graduate or internship program.
(23) Contact us
(24) For help submitting an advertisement or more information, contact the Industry Development Team: P (02) 8627 8408 E careers.administration@sydney.edu.aucareers.administration[at]sydney.edu.au.

(15) He compared taking a pain-killer for a headache before a cause is known with taking iron pills for anemia  while bleeding continues undiscovered.
(16) He added, "You are treating the result, not the cause."
(17) A headache is important also because it affects normal, happy living.
(18) The employee with a headache does less work.
(19) In a flash of temper he may upset his fellow workers or customers, causing a direct or indirect loss to his organization.
(20) The mother with a headache suffers and disturbs the family.

a|true|high23913.txt.passage:
(1) Why do people cross their fingers when facing difficulties-eve non-religious people?
(2) Why do we blame the black cat when we saw the low score of the test?
(3) These habits have been referred to as magical thinking and can be found everywhere in daily life.
(4) For example, a person wishes something bad for an annoying colleague.
(5) Or parents pray for a safe fight when seeing their son off.
(6) Psychologically helpful
(7) In examining these habits, scientists decided to search for a reason for humans' belief in magical powers.
(8) Psychologists have their own approach to the question.
(9) "I think in part it's because we are constantly exposed to our own thoughts, and thus likely to overestimate their connection to outside events."said Emily Pronin, a psychologist in the prefix = st1 /US.
(10) For people who are uncertain of their own abilities or slow to take action, magical thinking can be a great help, explained Dr. Daniel Wegner, a professor psychology at Harvard.
(11) Feeling that their own thoughts can control things can help people fight depression.
(12) A trait gained through evolution
(13) But evolutionists have their own ideas.
(14) Two schools of evolutionary thought have come up with reasons by analyzing the development of humans.
(15) They are the "adaptationists "and the "byproduct theorists ".
(16) The adaptationists say that belief in magical powers makes people feel better, less worried about difficulties, more focused on the future, and more willing to care for themselves.
(17) People with a strong faith (in something) have a better chance of surviving a tough environment or competition.
(18) But in byproduct theory,magical thinking is a product of our psychological features.
(19) "We automatically look for an explanation of why things happen," explains Justin Barrett, a psychologist.
(20) The brain, therefore, has evolved to make quick judgments about causation .
(21) So we often link two events based on nothing more than coincidence.
(22) For example, "I was just thinking about looking up my old high school girlfriend when she suddenly called me. "
(23) Another psychological feature is the theory of mind.
(24) We recognize that there could be an unseen power of mind affecting the outcome of an incident.
(25) Byproduct theorists claim that because of these features, we are born with a tendency to believe in our magical thinking.
(26) But we need to confine our magical thinking, warn psychologists.
(27) For most people, beliefs are just some sort of comforting private ritual.
(28) When something important is at stake, such as a test or a performance or a relationship, people don't simply perform their private rituals.
(29) They have to prepare.

a|true|high14089.txt.passage:
(1) I'm usually fairly skeptical about any research that concludes that people are either happier or unhappier or more or less certain of themselves than they were 50 years ago.
(2) While any of these statements might be true, they are practically impossible to prove scientifically.
(3) Still, I was struck by a report which concluded that today's children are significantly more anxious than children in the 1950s.
(4) In fact, the analysis showed, normal children aged 9 to 17 exhibit a higher level of anxiety today than children who were treated for mental illness 50 years ago.
(5) Why are America's kids so stressed?
(6) The report cites two main causes: increasing physical isolation  brought on by high divorce rates and less involvement in community, among other things and a growing perception that the world is a more dangerous place.
(7) Considering that we can't turn the clock back, adults can still do plenty to help the next generation.
(8) At the top of the list is nurturing  a better appreciation of the limits of individualism.
(9) No child is an island.
(10) Strengthening social ties helps build communities and protect individuals against stress.
(11) To help kids build stronger connections with others, you can pull the plug on TVs and computers.
(12) Your family will thank you later.
(13) They will have more time for facetoface relationships, and they will get more sleep.
(14) Limit the amount of virtual  violence your children are exposed to.
(15) It's not just video games and movies; children see a lot of murder and crime on the local news.
(16) Keep your expectations for your children reasonable.
(17) Many highly successful people never attended Harvard or Yale.
(18) Make exercise part of your daily routine.
(19) It will help you cope with your own anxieties and provide a good model for your kids.
(20) Sometimes anxiety is unavoidable.
(21) But it doesn't have to ruin your life.

(1) From March 29 to April 19, a new version of Death of a Salesman was performedat tne Capital Theatre as the first in a series   of foreign classics to mark the 60th anniversary of the Beijing People's Art Theatre.
(2) The last time this play was performed in Beijing was 29 years ago.
(3) Death of a Salesman was created in 1949 by Arthur Miller, who  used it to reveal   the weakness of the "American Dream" .
(4) The main character, Willy Loman, believes wholeheartedly in American capitalism, and desires to "succeed" but his own greed destroys him in the end.
(5) Death of a Salesman's first performance was a huge hit on Broadway, where it shocked the American theatre world, and strengthened the 33-year-old Miller's status as a master of American theatre.
(6) The play won the Pulitzer Prize for Drama, New York Drama Critics' Circle, and the Tony Award for Best Play.
(7) The New York Times praised Death of a Salesman as a milestone of 20th century theatre.
(8) In 1999, Death of a Salesman received another Tony Award for Best Revival, and the then 83-year-old Miller was given the National Medal of Arts.
(9) The 1983 version of Death of a Salesman was directed personally by Arthur Miller on a visit to China, with Beijing director and actor Ying Ruocheng as the main character of Willy Loman.
(10) On May 7, 1983, the play was performed for the first time at the Capital Theatre, and the first series of performances lasted until August 18, with over 50 performances, some of which filled the house, and all of which had great influence.
(11) When it was first performed here, China didn't have "salesman" , so the actors had to use their imagination to represent the characters and American society, and the audiences weren't entirely sure about some information.
(12) For example, they couldn't understand what "fixed payments" were, or why, if Willy was a lower-class member of American society, his family had a house, car, refrigerator, and television.
(13) But this didn't stop the play from becoming a widely-known page in Beijing's theatre history.

a|true|high22007.txt.passage:
(1) About 10 years ago, I started a job as a trainer for a telemarketing agency.
(2) In one of my first classes, the head trainer told a very inspiring story.
(3) He began by drawing a stickman standing in the middle of a circle.
(4) To make it more interesting, he drew things like a house, a car, and a few friends inside the circle.
(5) He asked the question, "Can anyone tell me what this is?"
(6) In a long silence, one guy decided to throw out "the world?"
(7) The trainer said, "That's close and this is your Comfort Zone.
(8) Inside your circle you have all the things that are important to you. "
(9) "Your home, your family, your friends, and your job.
(10) People feel that inside this circle they are safe from any danger or conflict. "
(11) "Can anyone tell me what happens when you step out of this circle?"
(12) A strong silence came over the room.
(13) The same eager guy abruptly announced, "You are afraid."
(14) Another guy said, "You make mistakes."
(15) The silence continued and the trainer smiled and said, "When you make mistakes, what can the result be?"
(16) The first guy shouted, "You learn something."
(17) "Exactly, you are learning."
(18) The trainer turned to the board and drew an arrow pointing from the stickman directly to the outside of the circle.
(19) He continued to say, "When you leave your Comfort Zone you put yourself out there, in front of the world to be in situations that you are not comfortable with."
(20) "The end result is that you have learned something that you did not know and you expand your knowledge to become a better person."
(21) He turned again to the board and drew a bigger circle around the original circle, and added a few new things like more friends, a bigger house, etc.
(22) "The moral of the story is that if you stay inside your Comfort Zone, you will never be able to expand your horizons and learn.
(23) When you step out of your Comfort Zone you will eventually make your circle bigger and your mind grow stronger, and all in all a better person."

(1) Medicine comes in lots of different packages.
(2) Painkillers in a tablet can make your headache go away.
(3) Antibiotic cream   from a tube can prevent your cuts from becoming infected.

(5) A team of scientists from Scotland say "yes".
(6) They've engineered special chickens that lay eggs with disease-treating drugs inside.
(7) These eggs come from chickens that have been engineered to produce certain drugs inside their egg whites.
(8) These drugs are made of molecules called proteins .
(9) Animals make thousands of proteins -- they're the main element in skin, hair, milk and meat.
(10) Since animals can make proteins easily, they're good candidates   for making protein drugs.
(11) Researchers have already made cows, sheep and goats produce protein drugs in their milk.
(12) But chickens are cheaper to take care of, need less room, and grow faster than these other animals.
(13) Those qualities could make chickens a better choice to become living drug factories, says Simon Lillico of the Roslin Institute near Edinburgh, Scotland.
(14) Lillico and a team of researchers changed chickens' DNA -- the code that tells cells how to make proteins -- so that the birds' cells made two protein drugs.
(15) One drug can treat skin cancer, and the other treats a nerve disease called multiple sclerosis  .
(16) The scientists changed the chickens' DNA so that the birds made these drugs only in their egg whites.
(17) This protects the chickens' bodies from the drugs' possible harmful effects and makes it easy for scientists to collect the drugs.

d0|notTrue|high23123.txt.passage:
(1) NOT all math skills are learned in the classroom.
(2) Some of them come naturally.
(3) Consider the split-second calculations  you make when you estimate   the number of empty seats on the school bus or the number of cookies in a cookie jar.
(4) These estimates can often be done without counting.
(5) That's because humans are born with the ability to closely guess the number of items in a group.
(6) Researchers call this ability a person's "number sense".
(7) To see how a person's inborn number sense might be linked to mathematical performance in the classroom, psychologist Justin Halberda and his colleagues from Johns Hopkins University in prefix = st1 /Baltimore,USran some tests.
(8) The scientists asked 64 14-year-olds to look at images   of yellow and blue dots that flashed on a computer screen for a fraction   of a second.
(9) Each image contained between 10 and 32 dots that varied in size.
(10) Some images contained twice as many blue dots  as yellow dots.
(11) In other images, however, the number of blue and yellow dots was nearly equal.
(12) For each image, the students were asked to estimate which color1 had more dots.
(13) The scientists found a wide variation in how well students could pick the color1 with the most dots.
(14) The scientists then looked at the students' math scores dating back to kindergarten.
(15) Children that performed best in the image test also scored the highest in standard math tests.
(16) The same finding held true at the other end of the spectrum  .
(17) Students who didn't score well on the image test received lower math scores.
(18) The study was the first to show a link between a person's inborn number sense and his or her achievement in formal math training.
(19) Does this connection mean that one cannot be good in math if they have a weak number sense?
(20) Or that having a strong number sense is a guarantee for good grades in math?
(21) The answers are not clear.
(22) While scientists continue looking at the possible links between a person's number sense and math achievement, one thing is certain: Doing lots of math homework will increase your chances of success.

a|notTrue|high16026.txt.passage:
(1) Simona lives in Bucharest, Romania.
(2) She is nine years old and HIV positive .
(3) When Simona's mother found out about her illness five years ago, her first reaction was to tell everyone, looking for support.
(4) "That's when I found out who really was my friend, and who wasn't, "she said.
(5) "Quite a few people stopped visiting me and asked their children not to play with Simona any more.
(6) Because of this, quarrels began in my family with each trouble brought about by her diagnosis.
(7) My husband became more and more unfriendly toward me and toward Simona.
(8) He didn't understand how important it was for Simona to receive regular examinations by a doctor or why she needed proper treatment for her sickness."
(9) When her mother tried to enroll  Simona in a school, the teacher warned her that when other parents found out about her illness she would be shunned by the other children.
(10) Simona's mother looked for help and found a social worker from the Community Resource Center.
(11) The social worker helped her enroll Simona in another school and provided emotional, financial, and material support for the family.
(12) Simona's parents were able to ease the tension in the family.
(13) Her mother also now attends parenting classes and support groups at the Center.
(14) The Community Resource Center provides not only many needed resources, but also a place where women can gain skills and self-confidence.
(15) It provides children with a place where they are accepted.
(16) At the Center parents and children find a place where they can grow and find the support they need to stay together and care for each other.

   - The school in question is the one that Simona's mother tried to enroll her in, which is not explicitly stated but can be inferred from the context.

6. Conclusion
   - Sentence (9) states that the teacher warned Simona's mother that other parents would shun Simona if they found out about her illness, implying that Simona was not accepted immediately by the first school.
   - Sentence (9) alone is sufficient to refute the statement.

(1) Do you know how to play a game called "Musical Chairs"?
(2) It is easy to play and most people enjoy it.
(3) All you need are some chairs, some people and some way of making music.
(4) You may use a piano or any other musical instrument, if someone can play it.
(5) You may use a tape recorder.
(6) You can even use a radio.
(7) Put the chairs in a row.
(8) The chairs may be put in twos, back to back.
(9) A better way is to have the chairs in one row with each chair facing in the opposite direction to the chair next to it.
(10) The game is easy.
(11) When the music starts, the players walk round the chairs.
(12) Everyone goes in the same direction, of course, they should walk in time to the music.
(13) If the music is fast they should walk quickly.
(14) If the music is slow, they should walk slowly.
(15) The person playing music cannot see the people in the game.
(16) When the music stops, the players try to sit on the chairs.
(17) If a person cannot find a chair to sit on, he drops out.
(18) Then, before the music starts again, one chair must be taken away.
(19) When the music stops again, one more player will be out.. At last, there will be two players and one chair.
(20) The one who sits on the chair when the music stops is the winner.

a|notTrue|high20138.txt.passage:
(1) How To Apply for USA Passports Online
(2) If you live in the United States, and you're planning a trip abroad, it is important to apply for new passports at least 2 months in advance (to avoid extra charges for an expedited passport processing).
(3) You can now get the application for a passport online.
(4) Some directions are detailed below:
(5) Downloading the passport application: The USA passport application, Form DS-11, is available for download on the US Department of State's website.
(6) *The passport application form is a Portable Document Format (PDF) file, and your computer must have Adobe Acrobat Reader to open it.
(7) If you do not have Adobe Acrobat Reader, you must first download the Adobe Acrobat software.
(8) You can do so by clicking here.
(9) *If you would like to download a blank application,      click here.
(10) If you would like to fill out the application online, then print it,click here.
(11) Printing the passport application: When printing the passport application, there are several requirements that must be followed.
(12) *You must use standard paper that is sized 8 1/2 inches by 11 inches.
(13) *The printing must be clear and legible.
(14) *The paper must be a standard medium weight, matte white (not shiny), and ink must be black.
(15) *The printer's ink must be black and there may not be any holes or perforations in the paper.
(16) *After printing, make sure that the box for pasting on your passport photo is the correct size of 2 inches by 2 inches.
(17) *It is important to print the application on one-sided paper.
(18) An application that is printed on double-sided paper will not be accepted.
(19) *For more information on these guidelines, visit the US Department of State's website.
(20) Filling out the passport application: As stated above, there are two ways to fill out the passport application: 1) typing your answers into the online form 2) writing answers by hand, in black ink, or by use of a typewriter.
(21) In both cases, you will need to provide the following information.
(22) *Your full name
(23) *Age
(24) *Date of birth
(25) *Place of birth
(26) *Phone number
(27) *Hair color1
(28) *Social security number
(29) *Mailing address 
(30) *Permanent Address.
(31) *Occupation
(32) *Employer
(33) *Your parents' names and birth information
(34) *Your spouse's name and birth information
(35) *Any other names you have used previously
(36) *Your travel plans
(37) *Emergency contact information
(38) Signing the application: It is important that you do not sign the application until you take the oath administered only by an authorized passport acceptance agent.
(39) Submitting the passport application: Along with the completed passport application, you must submit the following:
(40) *Proof of US Citizenship
(41) *Proof of Identity
(42) *Two recent passport sized (2x2 inches) photographs
(43) *The passport application fee

   - Sentence (6) uses the pronoun "your" which refers to the computer, but the computer is not explicitly mentioned earlier in the passage.
   - Sentence (5) introduces the passport application, which is the antecedent of "it" in Sentence (6).

(28) The detail of Crusoe's battle for survival on a lonely island is so vivid that the reader of Robinson Crusoe accepts the product of the author's imagination for reality.

a|notTrue|high23129.txt.passage:
(1) When she looked ahead, Florence Chadwick saw nothing but a solid wall of fog.
(2) Her body was numb .She had been swimming for nearly sixteen hours.
(3) Already she was the first woman to swim the English Channel in both directions.
(4) Now, at age 34,her goal was to become the first woman to swim from Catalina Island to the California coast.
(5) On that fourth of July morning in 1952,the sea was like an ice bath and the fog was so thick that she could hardly see her support boats.
(6) Sharks swam toward her lone figure, only to be driven away by rifle shots.
(7) Against the frigid grip of the sea, she struggled on--hour after hour--while millions watched on national television.
(8) Alongside Florence in one of the boats , her mother and her trainer offered encouragement.
(9) They told her it wasn't much farther.
(10) But all she could see was fog.
(11) They urged her not to give up.
(12) But after 15 hours, she still felt she couldn’t go on.
(13) With only a half mile to go, she asked to be pulled out.
(14) Still thawing her chilled body several hours later, she told a reporter," Look, I'm not excusing myself, but if I could have seen land I might have made it."
(15) It was not fatigue or even the cold water that defeated her .
(16) It was the fog.
(17) She was unable to see her goal.
(18) Two months later, she tried again.
(19) This time ,despite the same dense fog, she swam with her faith intact and her goal clearly pictured in her mind.
(20) She knew that somewhere behind that fog was land and this time she made it!
(21) Florence Chadwick became the first woman to swim the Catalina Channel, eclipsing the men's record by two hours!

(1) Experts have put forward detailed plans for a tunnel to connect Taiwan with the Chinese mainland.
(2) The shortest proposed route would be 126 kilometers more than twice the length of the English Channel Tunnel.
(3) And the longest proposed route would be 207 kilometers.
(4) A recent conference in Xiamen, Fujian Province brought together more than 70 experts.
(5) The event was co-sponsored   by universities from Taiwan and the Chinese mainland.
(6) Fujian is the province where both proposed routes would begin.
(7) There is direct passenger access between the mainland and Taiwan by air and sea at the moment.
(8) Experts say that it is better to start research sooner rather than later, although there is a lack of government funding .
(9) There are no technical problems to build a Taiwan tunnel.
(10) But it will require an improved political relationship across the Straits .
(11) A professor of Tsinghua University said, "A special feature of huge projects is that the period of preparation is longer than the period of construction."
(12) For example, he said, the English Channel Tunnel took 14 years of planning and had been discussed for two centuries.
(13) And preparations for the huge Three Gorges Dam on the Yangtze River began in the 1950s.
(14) The Xiamen conference focused on the longest southern route, which would pass Jinmen and Penghu.
(15) The first stage of the new project could be a bridge to cross the five kilometers between Xiamen and Jinmen.
(16) This would mean that travelling from Xiamen to Jinmen by car would only take five minutes.
(17) The longest tunnel now being planned anywhere in the world is the 54-kilometer land tunnel to link Lyon in France with Turin in Italy.
(18) The tunnel will not be completed until 2015-2020.

(10) Many procrastinators tell themselves they are perfectionists who work best under pressure.

(12) The behavior of procrastinators often makes them feel ashamed, inconveniences others and annoys loved ones.

(16) Nevertheless, he suggests a couple of good ways to get through the task at hand.

(1) Teenagers are under a lot of pressure to be thin.

a|true|college528.txt.passage:
(1) Sloth may be seen as a sin, but some of history's most accomplished men were fond of lounging around.
(2) Leonardo da Vinci enjoyed napping.
(3) So did Albert Einstein and Winston Churchill.
(4) Richard Buckminster Fuller advocated taking 30-minute naps every six hours.
(5) He is reported to have abandoned the practice only because" his schedule conflicted with that of his business associates, who insisted on sleeping like other men."
(6) No one has yet proved a correlation between napping and artistic brilliance or professional success, but an intriguing study published this week in the Archives of Internal Medicine claims to find a link between daytime siestas and good health.
(7) A team of researchers led by Androniki Naska of the University of Athens Medical School and Dimitrios Trichopoulos of Harvard's School of Public Health followed over 23,000 Greek patients with no history of coronary disease, cancer or stroke, for an average of six years.
(8) Their conclusion: napping just might save your life.
(9) The study found that the group of adults who took siestas (defined as 30-minute naps) at least three times a week had a third fewer deaths from heart disease than an equivalent group who did not sleep at all during the day.
(10) The benefit was greater for men than for women.
(11) (Whether women benefited at all was hard to estimate as there were too few deaths among them during the course of the study.)
(12) It was also greater for working men than for those who had retired.
(13) However, a number of previous studies done in the Mediterranean and in parts of Central America (where siestas remain common) have come up with conflicting results, but Dr Naska and Dr Trichopoulos argue that those studies have often been flawed.
(14) The subjects in some, for example, had survived heart attacks and may therefore have benefited more from napping than healthy individuals do.
(15) Given that all of the subjects of this new study were Greek, could the much-celebrated Mediterranean diet deserve credit, rather than the siestas?
(16) The firm answer from Dr Trichopoulos is" No" .
(17) And he is in a good position to say so, for it was he who did the pioneering research that put olive oil and a plant-based diet on the scientific map in the first place.
(18) Unlike some other siesta studies, his was controlled for diet, smoking, exercise and other relevant variables.
(19) The earlier findings about the benefits of the Mediterranean diet are confirmed, he says, and napping seems to help on top of that.
(20) Before buying a sofa for the office, however, it might be wise to consider the possibility of selection bias.
(21) Dr Trichopoulos concedes that" Type A"  personalities, whose hard-working lives may make them prone to heart attacks, are also much less likely to take naps during the day.
(22) That bias might be skewing the study's results.
(23) Even so, he advises, " Take a nap if you can."

a|true|college990.txt.passage:
(1) My father was a foreman of a sugar-cane plantation in Rio Piedras, Puerto Rico.
(2) My first job was to drive the oxen that ploughed the cane fields.
(3) I would walk behind an ox, guiding him with a broomstick.
(4) For $ 1 a day, I worked eight hours straight, with no food breaks.
(5) It was very tedious work, but it prepared me for life and taught me many lasting lessons.
(6) Because the plantation owners were always watching us, I had to be on time every day and work as hard as I could.
(7) I've never been late for any job since.
(8) I also learned about being respectful and faithful to the people you work for.
(9) More important, I earned my pay; it never entered my mind to say I was sick just because I didn't want to work.
(10) I was only six years old, but I was doing a man's job.
(11) Our family needed every dollar we could make because my father never earned more than $ 18 a week.
(12) Our home was a three-room wood shack with a dirty floor and no toilet.
(13) Nothing made me prouder than bringing home money to help my mother, father, two brothers and three sisters.
(14) This gave me self-esteem, one of the most important things a person can have.
(15) When I was seven, I got work at a golf course near our house.
(16) My job was to stand down the fairway and spot the balls as they landed, so the golfers could find them.
(17) Losing a ball meant you were fired, so I never missed one.
(18) Some nights I would lie in bed and dreamt of making thousands of dollars by playing golf and being able to buy a bicycle.
(19) The more I dreamed, the more I thought.
(20) Why not?
(21) I made my first golf club out of guava limb and a piece of pipe.
(22) Then I hammered an empty tin can into the shape of a ball.
(23) And finally I dug two small holes in the ground and hit the ball back and forth.
(24) I practiced with the same devotion and intensity.
(25) I learned working in the field - except now I was driving golf balls with club, not oxen with a broomstick.

a|true|college1126.txt.passage:
(1) A strong rupee is giving policymakers a new sort of headache
(2) THE Indian finance ministry's mid-year review, released this week, sees the external sector as a silver lining around the country's huge fiscal deficit. "
(3) Buoyant" and "encouraging" are the words used to describe three consecutive quarters of current-account surplus--the first in a quarter-century.
(4) Add to that swelling foreign-exchange reserves and a stronger rupee, and some are arguing that it is time for drastic liberalisation of India's foreign-exchange regime.
(5) They could be disappointed.
(6) For most of the past decade, the nominal value of the rupee has been allowed to decline gently against the dollar, by about 5% a year, thus staying fairly steady in real terms.
(7) This year, however, it has been appreciating in real terms (and, since June, nominally as well).
(8) It would have done so more sharply had the central bank not been buying dollars with gusto.
(9) Exporters of manufactured goods, obsessed with price competition from China, are aghast at the rise--and at the prospect held out by some forecasters that a sustained boom in India's IT exports means it will continue.
(10) The rupee's recent strength is only partly related to India's prowess in software and the mushrooming of "business-process outsourcing" in such projects as call-centres.
(11) The chunky surplus on invisibles owes more to remittances: non-resident Indians, attracted by the stability of the rupee and its higher interest rates, have been moving their offshore deposits back home.
(12) Similarly, Indian companies are borrowing more in dollars without selling rupees forward to hedge repayments.
(13) The trade deficit, meanwhile, has been shrinking, as imports grow slowly.
(14) The inflows have boosted foreign-exchange reserves by some $20 billion this year, to $66 billion, or 12 months'-worth of imports.
(15) The size of this cushion has triggered some calls for further liberalisation of the labyrinthine foreign-exchange controls that India still maintains, despite the move in 1993 towards rupee convertibility for trade purposes.
(16) In recent months, some controls have duly been eased.
(17) It is now simpler, for example, for individuals to open foreign-currency bank accounts, and for travellers to get hold of foreign exchange.
(18) And non-resident Indians have been allowed to take out money acquired through inheritance, or from rents and dividends.
(19) Some commentators have taken all this as a harbinger of full capital-account convertibility.
(20) That is not on the cards.
(21) The experience of 1991, when India ran out of money, has left the central bank prone to caution--an approach it felt was vindicated by the East Asian crisis of 1997-98.
(22) With war in Iraq looming and a turbulent oil market, some risk aversion is understandable.
(23) India's fiscal deficit--some 10% of GDP and widening--is another reason for moving slowly.
(24) Just as one rating agency, Moody's, is considering upgrading India's external debt, another, Fitch, has warned that its local-currency rating is under threat.
(25) Nor is it certain that opening the capital account would mean a weaker rupee.
(26) It might even attract more capital inflows.
(27) As India's exporters are learning, convertibility is a two-way street.

3. Evaluate the Evidence

4. Check Sufficiency
   - Sentence (14) and (15) provide evidence that India has increased foreign exchange reserves and maintains foreign exchange controls, which can help manage foreign exchange market events.
   - Sentence (21) shows that India has learned from past crises and is cautious in managing foreign exchange market events, further supporting the idea that it can now handle adverse events better.
   - All three sentences are needed to confidently support the statement.

5. Anaphora Resolution
   - Sentence (14) uses no pronouns but refers to "India" throughout.
   - Sentence (15) uses no pronouns but refers to "India" throughout.
   - Sentence (21) uses no pronouns but refers to "India" throughout.

6. Conclusion
   - Sentence (14) indicates an increase in foreign exchange reserves, which can help tackle adverse events.
   - Sentence (15) suggests that India has foreign exchange controls in place, which can help manage foreign exchange market events.
   - Sentence (21) shows that India has learned from past crises and is cautious in managing foreign exchange market events, further supporting the idea that it can now handle adverse events better.
   - All three sentences are needed to confidently support the statement.

a|true|college3724.txt.passage:
(1) Most growing plants contain much more water than all other materials combined.
(2) C. R. Barnes has suggested that it is as proper to term the plant a water structure as to call a house composed mainly of brick a brick building.
(3) Certain it is that all essential processes of plant growth and development occur in water.
(4) The mineral elements from the soil that are usable by the plant must be dissolved in the soil solution before they can be taken into the root.
(5) They are carried to all parts of the growing plant and are built into essential plant materials while in a dissolved state.
(6) The carbon dioxide (CO2) from the air may enter the leaf as a gas but is dissolved in water in the leaf before it is combined with a part of the water to form simple sugars -- the base material from which the plant body is mainly built.
(7) Actively growing plant parts are generally 75 to 90 percent water.
(8) Structural parts of plants, such as woody stems no longer actively growing, may have much less water than growing tissues.
(9) The actual amount of water in the plant at any one time, however, is only a very small part of what passes through it during its development.
(10) The processes of photosynthesis, by which carbon dioxide and water are combined -- in the presence of chlorophyll  and with energy derived from light -- to form sugars, require that carbon dioxide from the air enter the plant.
(11) This occurs mainly in the leaves.
(12) The leaf surface is not solid but contains great numbers of minute openings, through which the carbon dioxide enters.
(13) The same structure that permits the one gas to enter the leaf, however, permits another gas -- water vapor -- to be lost from it.
(14) Since carbon dioxide is present in the air only in trace quantities (3 to 4 parts in 10,000 parts of air) and water vapor is near saturation in the air spaces within the leaf (at 80℉, saturated air would contain about 186 parts of water vapor in 10,000 parts of air), the total amount of water vapor lost is many times the carbon dioxide intake.
(15) Actually, because of wind and other factors, the loss of water in proportion to carbon dioxide intake may be even greater than the relative concentrations of the two gases.
(16) Also, not all of the carbon dioxide that enters the leaf is synthesized into carbohydrates .

   - Sentence (16): "Actually, not all of the carbon dioxide that enters the leaf is synthesized into carbohydrates." → directly contradicts the idea that all carbon dioxide is synthesized.

3. Evaluate the Evidence
   - Sentence (16) states that not all of the carbon dioxide that enters the leaf is synthesized into carbohydrates, contradicting the claim that all carbon dioxide is synthesized.

4. Check Sufficiency

   - Sentence (10) connects the carbon dioxide intake to the plant, grounding Sentence (16) in the context of the plant.
   - Both sentences are needed to fully support the statement.

5. Anaphora Resolution
   - Sentence (16) uses no pronouns but does rely on context to identify the carbon dioxide that enters the leaf.
   - Sentence (10) provides that context by introducing the carbon dioxide intake in the plant.

6. Conclusion
   - Sentence (16) states that not all carbon dioxide that enters the leaf is synthesized.
   - Sentence (10) confirms that the carbon dioxide intake is in the plant.
   - Both are needed to confidently determine that only part of the carbon dioxide in the plant is synthesized.

Answer: 2

a|true|college585.txt.passage:
(1) Neither Oliver Williamson of the University of California at Berkeley nor Elinor Ostrom of Indiana University at Bloomington was widely tipped to win this year's Nobel Prize for economics.
(2) This may be because their work sits at the boundary of economics, law and political science, and tackles different questions to the ones that economists have traditionally studied.
(3) Mr. Williamson and Ms. Ostrom work independently of each other but both have contributed plenty to economists' understanding of which institutions-firms, markets, governments, or informal systems of social norms, for example-are best suited for conducting different types of economic transactions.
(4) Ronald Coase, a British economist who won the Nobel Prize in 1991, argued that in some situations, and for some kinds of transactions, administrative decision-making within a single legal entity is more efficient than a straightforward market transaction.
(5) Mr. Coase's arguments were influential and convinced economists that the internal workings of organisations were worth paying attention to explicitly.
(6) But it was left to Mr. Williamson to refine Mr. Coase's theory and clarify what features of certain transactions made carrying them out more efficient within a firm rather than in the market.
(7) Mr. Williamson showed that complex transactions involving investment decisions that are much more valuable within a relationship than to a third party are best done within a firm.
(8) Part of the problem, he argued, was that some economic transactions are so complicated, and involve so many things which could go wrong, that writing a legally enforceable contract that takes all possibilities into account is impossible.
(9) Simpler transactions are completed easily in markets; more complicated ones may demand firms.
(10) But in later work he also showed that organising matters within companies had costs: in particular, it relied on internal authority to get things done, and this could be abused.
(11) Ms. Ostrom has concentrated on a different aspect of economic governance.
(12) She has spent her life studying how human societies manage common resources such as forests, rivers, pastures or wildlife.
(13) Just as with public goods, it is difficult to prevent people from using the commons.
(14) But unlike public goods, and like private ones, what one person takes leaves less for others.
(15) Economic theory then predicts that rational individuals will overuse these resources.
(16) Economists have tended to emphasise property rights as a solution to the problem of managing common resources.
(17) But Ms. Ostrom spent much of her early career studying how communities managed such common resources.
(18) She found that groups of people tended to have complex sets of rules, norms and penalties to ensure that such resources were used sustainably.
(19) Such self-governance often worked well.
(20) Successful informal institutions, she found, have certain features in common, which sets them apart from institutions that fail.
(21) The principles of game theory, particularly the theory of repeated interactions, proved remarkably useful in formulating general principles of how common resources ought to be managed without necessarily resorting to private or state ownership.
(22) Mr Williamson launched an entire branch of economic theorising which looks more deeply into firms than economists had tended to do previously.
(23) His theories have also helped with understanding the choice between equity and debt, and corporate finance more generally.
(24) Ms. Ostrom's research has spawned many experiments about how people interact strategically.
(25) Some of these have influenced game theory, which originally provided Ms. Ostrom with her analytical tools.

3. Evaluate the Evidence

   - The statement is supported by this sentence alone.

4. Check Sufficiency

   - No additional sentences are needed to support the statement.

5. Anaphora Resolution
   - Sentence (3) uses no pronouns but clearly refers to Williamson and Ostrom through their names.
   - The referents are unambiguous and central to the passage.

6. Conclusion
   - Sentence (3) states that both Williamson and Ostrom contribute to understanding economic transactions.
   - The sentence alone is sufficient to support the statement.

Answer: 1

a|true|college3016.txt.passage:
(1) The biographer has to dance between two shaky positions with respect to the subject.
(2) Too close a relation, and the writer may be objectivity.
(3) Not close enough, and the writer may lack the sympathy necessary to any effort to portray a mind, a soul-the quality of life.
(4) Who should write the biography of a family, for example?
(5) Because of their closeness to the subject, family members may have special information, but by the same token, they may not have the distance that would allow them to be fair.
(6) Similarly, a king's servant might not be the best one to write a biography of that king.
(7) But a foreigner might not have the knowledge and sympathy necessary to write the king's biography-not for a readership from within the kingdom, at any rate.
(8) There is no ideal position for such a task.
(9) The biographer has to work with the position he or she has in the world, adjusting that position as necessary to deal with the subject.
(10) Every position has strengths and weaknesses: to thrive, a writer must try to become aware of these, evaluate them in terms of the subject, and select a position accordingly.
(11) When their subjects are heroes or famous figures, biographies often reveal a democratic motive: they attempt to show that their subjects are only human, no better than anyone else.
(12) Other biographies are meant to change us, to invite us to become better than we are.
(13) The biographies of Jesus found in the Bible are in this class.
(14) Biographers may claim that their account is the "authentic" one.
(15) In advancing this claim, they are helped if the biography is "authorized" by the subject, this presumably allows the biographer special access to private information.
(16) "Unauthorized" biographies also have their appeal, however, since they can suggest an independence of mind in the biographer.
(17) In book promotions, the "unauthorized" characterisation usually suggests the prospect of juicy gossip that the subject had hoped to suppress.
(18) A subject might have several biographies, even several "authentic" ones.
(19) We sense intuitively that no one is in a position to tell the story of a life, perhaps not even the subject, and this has been proved by the history of biography.

   - Sentence (19) directly states that no one can tell the story of a life perfectly, making it sufficient to support the statement.

   - Sentence (19) uses no pronouns but refers to the history of biography, which is previously mentioned in the passage.

6. Conclusion
   - Sentence (8) implies that perfection is unattainable.
   - Sentence (19) directly states that no one can tell the story of a life perfectly, confirming the claim.
   - Sentence (19) is sufficient to support the statement.

Answer: 1

a|true|college578.txt.passage:
(1) Governments around the world are struggling to cope with the rising cost of health care, and of drugs in particular.
(2) Many rich countries have resorted to price controls, and some on the American left advocate them noisily.
(3) But drug firms maintain that America, where they are free to price patented pills largely as they please, is the engine of global pharmaceutical innovation, while price-controlling Europeans are free riders.
(4) That, says PhRMA, the industry's lobby based in Washington, D.C., is because price regulations seen in other rich countries" chill innovation, impede patients' access to the newest cutting-edge medicines, and trigger innovators to relocate to countries with more progressive public policy."
(5) A new study, written by Donald Light, a visiting professor at Stanford University, claims that European drug firms are more innovative than American ones, in spite of price controls.
(6) That flies in the face of an influential paper published in the same journal in 2006, which examined the geographic origins of drugs registered between 1982 and 2003 and concluded that favourable public policies had helped propel America to the top of the list.
(7) PhRMA has rushed to denounce Mr Light's study, insisting it gives a" distorted picture"  and understates the impact of" home-grown innovation" .
(8) The tiff exposes two fallacies that come from looking at a globalised industry through national spectacles.
(9) Both sides attribute new drugs to a specific country of origin (based on the location of the headquarters of the firm that first launches them).
(10) But Patricia Danzon of the Wharton Business School argues that this makes little sense when most big drug firms have laboratories in several countries and often acquire drugs under development from biotechnology firms located elsewhere.
(11) By the same token, firms develop drugs for the global market, observes Alan Garber of Stanford, not just for the local one.
(12) So the imposition of price caps in a given country will not necessarily quash innovation there, thanks to the lure of exports.
(13) The exception to this rule, of course, is the United States, which alone accounts for over 40% of global sales.
(14) So what would happen if America's Congress imposes price regulations?
(15) If the cuts are swinging, then the prize for inventors everywhere will be reduced.
(16) But short of such a radical scenario, pricing reform would not kill off innovation, according to Sanford Bernstein, a financial-research firm.
(17) It has analysed the likely impact of a cut of 20% in the prices Medicare, the government health scheme for the elderly, pays for its drugs.
(18) It concluded that the earnings per share of big pharmaceutical firms would drop by 3-8%.
(19) There is even reason to think that pricing reforms may boost innovation.
(20) Britain and Germany are pioneering comparative reviews of drugs' effectiveness and cost-benefit analyses aimed at reimbursing firms for new drugs based on how well they perform.
(21) Janssen-Cilag persuaded Britain's health service to accept Velcade, its expensive cancer drug, by offering a money-back guarantee if it did not work as well as promised.
(22) The American pharmaceutical lobby is violently opposed to making such an approach compulsory.
(23) Some suspect that its opposition stems from the fear that many expensive and profitable pills would be found to be of dubious value.
(24) But as Ms Danzon points out, " Comparative-effectiveness reviews are an indirect form of price control-but one that is consistent with encouraging innovation."

   - Sentence (22): "The American pharmaceutical lobby is violently opposed to making such an approach compulsory." → The industry actively engages in political action to maintain its current practices.

3. Evaluate the Evidence
   - Sentence (3) implies that the industry uses political freedom to its advantage, suggesting a connection between politics and self-interest.
   - Sentence (7) shows that PhRMA, the industry's lobby, is actively involved in politics to defend its interests, further supporting the statement.
   - Sentence (21) demonstrates that the industry may use political tactics to influence drug approval and pricing, reinforcing the idea that it utilizes politics for its own benefit.
   - Sentence (22) shows that the industry actively engages in political action to maintain its current practices, providing additional evidence.

   - Sentence (3) alone suggests that the industry may use political freedom to its advantage, but it does not explicitly state that it utilizes politics for its own benefit.
   - Sentences (7), (21), and (22) all provide evidence that the industry is involved in politics and uses political tactics to further its interests, making them necessary to support the statement.

5. Anaphora Resolution
   - Sentences (7), (21), and (22) use the pronoun "it" to refer to the American pharmaceutical industry, which is clearly defined in Sentence (1).

6. Conclusion
   - Sentences (3), (7), (21), and (22) all provide evidence that the American pharmaceutical industry is involved in politics and uses political tactics to further its interests, supporting the statement that it utilizes politics for its own benefit.

a|true|college543.txt.passage:
(1) The American military tribunals set up to pass judgment on terrorism suspects at Guantánamo Bay lurched into life this week when David Hicks, the" Australian Taliban" , unexpectedly pleaded guilty.
(2) The Pentagon will be relieved that the tribunals have started to show results after five years of controversy over the status of" enemy combatants" , claims of torture, the admissibility of forced confessions and a Supreme Court ruling last year that halted an earlier version of the tribunals.
(3) Yet the Hicks case is hardly an impressive start for America's offshore justice.
(4) Critics say the 31-year-old Mr Hicks, a former kangaroo skinner and alleged al-Qaeda fighter, faces a" kangaroo court" .
(5) Two of his civilian lawyers were barred from the hearing on March 26th.
(6) His military lawyer, Major Michael Mori, has been threatened with prosecution for harshly criticising the tribunals.
(7) The prosecutor, Colonel Morris Davis, said the defence lawyer may have breached military law that bans officers from using" contemptuous words"  against the president or senior officials.
(8) Major Mori filed a counter-charge against the prosecution, saying it was trying to intimidate him.
(9) In the end the motion will not be heard because Mr Hicks, looking pale and bedraggled, admitted the charge of" providing material support for terrorism" .
(10) A charge of" attempted murder in violation of the laws of war"  was dropped.
(11) As The Economist went to press, the tribunal was due to hear details of his plea and pass sentence, which Mr Hicks is expected to serve out in Australia.
(12) His father, Terry Hicks, said his son had had" five years of absolute hell"  since being captured in Afghanistan and allegedly suffering beatings, rape and forced injections in American custody-accusations rejected by the Pentagon.
(13) The guilty plea was just" a way to get home" , said Mr Hicks's father.
(14) Many in Australia regard Mr Hicks as more of a lost soul than a dangerous terrorist.
(15) Indeed, his charge sheet portrays him as little more than an al-Qaeda foot-soldier, and a poor one at that.
(16) His jihadi CV is pitiful compared with the evidence being given by some of the 14" high value detainees"  belatedly brought to Guantánamo from CIA secret prisons in September.
(17) They include al-Qaeda's operational chief, Khalid Sheikh Mohammed, who in a closed hearing to determine whether he was an" enemy combatant"  earlier this month boasted that he had organised the September 11th attacks" from A to Z" , and 30 other plots.
(18) Meanwhile, Ahmed Ghalfan Ghailani and Waleed bin Atttash, from Tanzania and Yemen respectively, have admitted supplying equipment for the bombing of the American embassies in Kenya and Tanzania in 1998.
(19) Many of these big names will face trial, and perhaps the death penalty.
(20) But the tribunals are expected to try only 60-80 of the more than 380 prisoners currently in Guantánamo.
(21) Over the years hundreds of detainees have been sent back to their countries, where some have been jailed and most have been released-and sometimes re-arrested.
(22) The Bush Administration says it wants to phase out and eventually close the jail.
(23) But it is unlikely to do so for some years, either because it lacks the evidence to prosecute detainees (even under the more lax military rules it is using), or because other countries are unwilling to take them back.
